fields.title: Railways Reimagined: Reliability, Automation, and Equitable Connectivity
fields.description: Rail systems can improve urban-suburban connectivity by making transport more frequent, reliable, and efficient. Highlighting the role of rail in reducing congestion, pollution, and pressure on crowded city centers, while also supporting easier movement for people and goods. It also emphasizes the importance of technology, strong governance, and sustainable funding, while ensuring that public transport remains accessible and equitable.

fields.title: Driving Inclusivity and Accessibility in Rail Transportation
fields.description: Nancy Chan's work as a project manager specializing in agile delivery for transportation and digital twin projects, particularly her efforts to improve rail accessibility. It addresses a major issue in the rail industry: the lack of consistent data on how well stations serve passengers with disabilities. To help solve this, the project involves auditing 2,572 stations and collecting reliable information across all public areas. This data helps decision-makers identify where investments and improvements are most needed. The discussion also highlights the importance of making rail networks more accessible, as it allows people to feel more connected to society, family, and work opportunities. Chan also emphasizes the value of diversity and inclusivity in engineering, noting that greater representation can inspire a more positive future for the next generation.

fields.title: Philippine's Transportation Projects
fields.description: The Philippines' ongoing transportation transformation, which aims to address long-standing issues such as poor infrastructure, heavy traffic congestion, and weak connectivity. Major investments are being made in railways, road networks, and sustainable technologies to improve mobility, support economic growth, and enhance the quality of life for citizens. Key projects include the North-South Commuter Railway, which will connect Manila to nearby provinces and major hubs, along with other railway expansions such as the Mindanao Railway, PNR Bicol South Line, and MRT-4. Road projects like the Luzon Spine Expressway Network and the Pan-Philippine Highway are also being developed to reduce travel time, improve logistics, and strengthen connections across the country. The summary also highlights the use of intelligent transportation systems, electric vehicles, and smart city developments like New Clark City as part of the country's shift toward a more modern, efficient, and sustainable transportation system.

fields.title: Railway Bridges over Philippines
fields.description: The Philippines' major railway development efforts aimed at reducing severe traffic congestion and supporting national economic growth. It highlights how Metro Manila's traffic causes billions of pesos in daily losses, pushing the government to invest heavily in rail infrastructure through programs such as Build, Build, Build. Major projects include MRT-7, the North-South Commuter Railway, the Subic-Clark Railway, the Metro Manila Subway, and the South Long Haul Project, all designed to improve passenger mobility, freight movement, and regional connectivity. These railway projects are seen as important because trains offer faster, more reliable, higher-capacity, and more environmentally sustainable transport compared to road-based systems. Despite delays caused by the COVID-19 pandemic, these projects are expected to significantly improve transportation, strengthen economic activity, and help reduce poverty in the coming years.

fields.title: Social Innovation in Transport and Mobility
fields.description: Hitachi's vision for social innovation in transport through integrated door-to-door mobility, where different transport modes are connected into one seamless system using tools such as smartphone ticketing, data-sharing, and smart control systems. It highlights Hitachi's high-speed rail link from Kent to London, which reduced travel time and improved commuters' daily lives. This approach can also be applied to Philippine railways by using modern rail technology, integrated ticketing, and real-time transport data to make travel faster, more reliable, and more convenient. Similar improvements could help reduce traffic congestion, support economic growth, and improve the quality of life for Filipino commuters.

fields.title: Rail Transport Basics
fields.summary: Core ideas about trains, tracks, passenger movement, freight, and why rail is efficient for dense cities.

fields.title: Philippine Rail Systems
fields.summary: MRT, LRT, PNR, subway, commuter rail, and regional railway projects that shape Philippine mobility.

fields.title: Accessibility, Mobility & The Economy
fields.summary: How rail systems reduce congestion, connect people to opportunity, and support productivity, equity, and urban growth.

fields.title: Gallery Snapshot
fields.summary: Images and videos of Philippine rail systems, station maps, commuters, construction, and global rail references.

fields.title: Rail Transport History
fields.summary: External reading on how rail transport developed and why it became central to urban and regional mobility.

fields.title: World Bank: Livable Cities
fields.summary: A source connecting urban mobility investment with more livable, accessible, and sustainable cities.

fields.title: Rail Transport Supports Economic Development
fields.excerpt: Railways drive economic growth, improve property values, and support sustainable urban development in the Philippines.
fields.raw_text:
Railway systems are simply not just transport solutions, they drive economic growth, increase property value, and encourage commercial development (Barham, 2025). Efficient transportation is a cornerstone of economic development, especially in urban areas. Among the various modes of transport, railways play a unique role in linking people, goods, and businesses, helping cities grow while supporting sustainable development (World Bank Group, 2016).

The use of rail as a means of transport is reliable, economical, and eco-friendly when moving both people and goods. Railways have an advantage over road transport since they have more capacity and produce less pollution compared to the latter (Effects of transportation on the economy, n.d.). Railways connect urban areas to industries and other neighboring countries, creating job opportunities and business ventures (World Bank Group, 2016).

Countries in the world have learned that strategic rail transport is key to improving connectivity. This could include connecting rural areas to economic zones or cities, facilitating border crossing, and making inter-city connections more efficient (Barham, 2025). Railways help cities and regions become more efficient and productive. Japan has an excellent railway system in the form of its bullet train system, which makes it possible to move around the country quickly, improving commuting, economic efficiency, and tourism (Barham, 2025).

There are other benefits of rail transport in cities in terms of improving lives. Commuter trains, light rail transport, and metros all help reduce traffic congestion, make commuting faster, and increase employment opportunities (Effects of transportation on the economy, n.d.). In addition, cities with good rail systems have thriving downtowns and higher land prices. By providing reliable transport solutions, rail transport helps facilitate sustainable urban growth. Rail transport enables the development of transit-oriented cities that do not depend on cars for commuting (World Bank Group, 2016).

Furthermore, railways help stimulate tourism. They make it easier for tourists to visit destinations, museums, and natural attractions. Accessible urban centers support local businesses and create employment in the service sector, thus boosting the local economy (Barham, 2025).

The Philippines, with its growing urban population and expanding economic hubs, stands to benefit from strategic rail investments. Linking Metro Manila to nearby cities, connecting ports with industrial areas, or developing regional rail corridors can reduce congestion, improve trade efficiency, and support inclusive growth (World Bank Group, 2016; Effects of transportation on the economy, n.d.). By learning from international examples while adapting solutions to local needs, rail infrastructure can become a key driver of sustainable urban and regional development (Barham, 2025).

sources.label: Barham, D. (2025, September 18). How modern rail networks can boost economic growth and regional trade in Africa | mobility | Vuka group. How Modern Rail Networks Can Boost Economic Growth and Regional Trade in Africa.
Effects of transportation on the economy. (n.d.).
World Bank Group. (2016). Railways. In World Bank.

fields.title: What are the Philippine Railway System Latest Projects?
fields.excerpt: The Philippines is expanding major rail projects like the Metro Manila Subway, NSCR, and Mindanao Railway to improve connectivity, reduce congestion, and support economic growth.
fields.raw_text:
In recent years, the Philippines has undertaken numerous large-scale railway initiatives. The purpose of each project is to modernize movement across the country, relieve traffic congestion, and link the different areas of the Philippines. New railway projects represent a renewed commitment to high-quality mass transport infrastructure by either having construction underway, active design documentation, or government officials pushing for construction to start (De Villa, 2026).

The Metro Manila Subway will be the first rapid transit underground in the country and will run for about 33 km, ultimately reaching Ninoy Aquino International Airport from Valenzuela City. Purposefully, the metro subway project will attempt to reduce the extreme traffic problems currently experienced in the National Capital Region (NCR) (GMA Integrated News, 2026). Although there are many challenges for project construction, including acquiring land and building an underground facility, the Department of Transportation hopes to schedule demonstrational rides on the new subway system by 2028 with full operations expected by 2031-2032 depending on the funding available to clear right-of-ways for the metro subway. The subway will also connect to both LRTs and MRTs and the North-South Commuter Railway (De Villa, 2026).

The North-South Commuter Railway (NSCR) is one of the biggest projects in Southeast Asia, and aims to provide rail access across both Metro Manila and parts of Luzon (Pampanga, Laguna). It will span approximately 147 km, starting at Clark in Pampanga and running through Metro Manila to connect to Calamba in Laguna. The NSCR will be developed in multiple phases and is currently under construction; the South Commuter section of the project, which runs from Blumentritt to Calamba (approximately 54.6 km), is being developed with assistance from both the ADB and JICA (Bank, 2023). Once completed, the NSCR will reduce travel times drastically, improve access to Metro Manila's rail system, and provide daily transportation for hundreds of thousands of commuters (De Villa, 2026).

While infrastructure improvements on Luzon are being actively developed, the Mindanao Railway Project is still in the planning and construction phase. The Mindanao Railway is intended to be a multi-phase integrated transit system that will provide greater public transportation access to residents of Mindanao and foster economic development in rural areas. Lawmakers such as Senate Minority Leader Alan Peter Cayetano have requested that the Department of Transportation pursue the development and funding of the Mindanao Railway project and have linked this request to the larger objectives of improved access and economic development in rural areas (Cayetano urges DOTr, 2025).

Together, these railway projects reflect a strategic shift toward modern, high-capacity transport infrastructure:

Greater connectivity: Once operational, Philippines rail networks will link urban and regional centers more efficiently, helping commuters travel faster and with less stress (De Villa, 2026).

Economic Impact: Rail infrastructure typically supports jobs during construction and operation while attracting investments around stations and corridors (Effects of transportation on the economy, n.d.).

Environmental Benefits: Shifting commuters from roads to rails can reduce traffic congestion and carbon emissions over the long term (Effects of transportation on the economy, n.d.).

sources.label: De Villa, K. (2026, April 12). Hopes on track: How are PH railway projects going? INQUIRER.net.
GMA Integrated News. (2026, January 24). DOTr eyes Metro Manila Subway demo run in Q1 2028. GMA News Online.
Bank, A. D. (2023, March 7). ADB approves project financing for South Commuter Railway project in the Philippines. Asian Development Bank.
Cayetano urges DOTr to revive Mindanao Railway, push rural development. (2025, November 29). Senate of the Philippines.
Effects of transportation on the economy. (n.d.). National Geographic Education.

fields.title: ASEAN Rail Systems vs the Philippine Rail System
fields.excerpt: By comparing ASEAN rail systems with the Philippines, this blog shows how better rail networks can improve daily mobility, tourism, and long-term growth.
fields.raw_text:
Rail transport has long been a cornerstone of mobility in Southeast Asia. Countries like Thailand, Malaysia, and Singapore have established efficient, modern rail networks that connect urban and rural areas, facilitate trade, and support tourism (Gucilatar, 2017). Meanwhile, the Philippines continues to lag behind, facing systemic infrastructure challenges that limit mobility and economic potential (Daily Guardian, 2025; Francisco, 2025).

Across Southeast Asia, rail networks demonstrate variety and efficiency. Thailand features multiple train classifications, from super express to regular services, connecting cities like Bangkok, Chiang Mai, and Nong Khai (Travelfish). The network is not only a transportation system but also a cultural experience, offering scenic journeys that attract travelers. Malaysia and Singapore have high-speed rail and modern commuter systems linking major urban centers, supporting daily commuters and cross-border travel efficiently (Gucilatar, 2017). Indonesia's Java rail network provides extensive commuter and intercity connections, while other islands are gradually developing or reopening their rail systems, signaling regional commitment to rail-based mobility (Travelfish). Vietnam and Cambodia also support intercity travel, with Vietnam investing in modernizing routes connecting Hanoi, Saigon, and surrounding provinces (Gucilatar, 2017).

The key strengths of ASEAN rail systems include high-density networks, modern trains, multiple service classes, and integrated ticketing options. These elements contribute to reliability and positive traveler experiences (Travelfish; Gucilatar, 2017).

In contrast, the Philippines ranks last in ASEAN for overall transport infrastructure quality and performance (Daily Guardian, 2025; Francisco, 2025). According to PIDS, the country's railway system is severely underdeveloped, with a railway density of only 1.52 kilometers per square kilometer and a low infrastructure quality score of 1.9 out of 7. Most operational lines are concentrated in Metro Manila, and intercity rail connections remain sparse (Francisco, 2025). Budget allocations reflect these challenges. Despite prioritization in the Department of Transportation (DOTr) budget, actual capital outlay for rail systems remains minimal, heavily outweighed by road infrastructure investment (Francisco, 2025). Ongoing projects such as the North-South Commuter Railway, Mindanao Railway, and Metro Manila Subway aim to expand capacity, but nationwide coverage is still limited (Francisco, 2025). Operational issues such as aging trains, frequent delays, and inadequate maintenance further hinder service quality. Unlike ASEAN neighbors, the Philippines has yet to develop high-speed intercity rail or integrated commuter networks capable of supporting both urban and regional travel (Daily Guardian, 2025; Francisco, 2025).

ASEAN trains provide multiple service classes, punctual schedules, modern rolling stock, integrated ticketing, and scenic routes, all of which enhance reliability and passenger experience (Travelfish; Gucilatar, 2017). Philippine trains, in contrast, face overcrowding, frequent service disruptions, and aging infrastructure. Even metro lines operate beyond capacity, creating daily commuter challenges (Francisco, 2025).

Tourism integration is another differentiator. In Thailand and Vietnam, trains double as tourist attractions, offering scenic journeys (Travelfish). Philippine railway travel remains largely utilitarian and underutilized for tourism, despite government plans to improve inter-island connectivity (Francisco, 2025).

The Philippine Development Plans from 2001 to 2028 outline a gradual shift toward rail modernization. Infrastructure expansion projects include the North-South Commuter Railway, Mindanao Railway, and Metro Manila Subway, aimed at extending coverage across major islands (Francisco, 2025). The government also plans to integrate freight and passenger rail with ports and dry cargo terminals to support trade and reduce road congestion (Francisco, 2025). Modernization initiatives include upgrading rolling stock, adopting integrated ticketing, and collaborating with private and multilateral partners to increase efficiency and innovation (Francisco, 2025).

A multimodal approach is also emphasized, with investments in buses, ferries, RORO vessels, and airports complementing rail development to improve mobility nationwide (Gucilatar, 2017; Francisco, 2025). Despite ambitious plans, progress is constrained by fragmented budgeting, lack of centralized infrastructure data, and delayed project implementation (Francisco, 2025).

Success will require sustained investment, better planning, and coordinated execution across government agencies (Daily Guardian, 2025; Francisco, 2025). ASEAN rail systems illustrate how strategic investment and modern infrastructure can facilitate urbanization, tourism, and trade (Gucilatar, 2017; Travelfish). The Philippines, while acknowledging these benefits and planning aggressively, continues to face significant gaps in coverage, quality, and operational efficiency. Realizing the potential of Philippine rail transport will require not only increased funding but also integrated planning, data-driven decision-making, and effective coordination between agencies (Daily Guardian, 2025; Francisco, 2025).
body_paragraphs: Rail transport has long been a cornerstone of mobility in Southeast Asia. Countries like Thailand, Malaysia, and Singapore have established efficient, modern rail networks that connect urban and rural areas, facilitate trade, and support tourism (Gucilatar, 2017). Meanwhile, the Philippines continues to lag behind, facing systemic infrastructure challenges that limit mobility and economic potential (Daily Guardian, 2025; Francisco, 2025).

Across Southeast Asia, rail networks demonstrate variety and efficiency. Thailand features multiple train classifications, from super express to regular services, connecting cities like Bangkok, Chiang Mai, and Nong Khai (Travelfish). The Philippines, while acknowledging these benefits and planning aggressively, continues to face significant gaps in coverage, quality, and operational efficiency. sources.label: Francisco, K. A. (2025, January 6). Transport infrastructure in the Philippines: From plans to actual allocation [Discussion paper].
Gucilatar, T. (2017, April 20). Traveling in ASEAN: Transport systems in Southeast Asia. Rappler.
PH ranks last in ASEAN transport infrastructure. (2025, March 18). Daily Guardian.
Trains in Southeast Asia. (n.d.). Travelfish.

fields.title: First-Hand Stories and Blogs About Daily Train Use
fields.excerpt: Metro Manila commuters describe how MRT, LRT, and PNR shape daily travel, from overcrowding and station access to the small conveniences that make trips manageable.
fields.raw_text:
BLOG 3: First-Hand Stories and Blogs About Daily Train Use

Picture: first-hand-stories-and-blogs-about-daily-train-use.jpeg
https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:1400/format:webp/1*QyrQEOjOy5TIwhkOwhPP_Q.jpeg

Navigating Metro Manila often requires the use of the Metro Rail Transit (MRT) and Light Rail Transit (LRT), which have become essential for daily commuters. These rail systems offer a faster alternative to the heavily congested streets of EDSA, allowing people to reach their destinations efficiently despite frequent overcrowding. The MRT operates along thirteen stations from North Avenue to Taft Avenue, while the LRT is divided into two lines: LRT-1 with nineteen stations and LRT-2 with eleven stations. Each line has distinct features, including the MRT’s blue trains and the LRT’s yellow trains, which make them easily identifiable (Buduan, 2018).

Commuting experiences vary depending on the line and time of day. LRT-2 generally provides the most comfortable rides due to its spacious trains and well-maintained stations. In contrast, MRT and LRT-1 frequently experience severe overcrowding, especially during peak hours. First-time commuters often find stations such as Katipunan or Doroteo Jose difficult to navigate because of unclear signage and complex layouts. Despite these challenges, the speed and reliability of the trains make them a more practical choice than buses, which often become trapped in heavy traffic (Buduan, 2018).

Daily commuting also presents additional difficulties. Elevators and escalators are often out of service, and essential amenities such as toilets or Wi-Fi are frequently unavailable. Many passengers mitigate these issues by using Beep cards, which simplify payment and reduce time spent in long queues. These small conveniences make a noticeable difference for commuters who endure crowded conditions regularly, helping them complete their journeys with greater efficiency (Top Gear Philippines, 2019).

The train systems of Metro Manila are used not only for short city trips but also for longer commutes to surrounding provinces. Many commuters rely on the Philippine National Railway (PNR) for journeys between Manila and nearby towns to avoid heavy traffic and reduce travel costs. While these trips can be crowded and offer limited amenities, they provide a predictable and efficient way to manage time and schedules. Commuters often adapt to these conditions, finding ways to occupy themselves, reflect, or plan their day during the journey (Castillo, 2019).

Together, the MRT, LRT, and PNR illustrate both the challenges and resilience of daily commuting in Metro Manila. Riders from different socioeconomic backgrounds share the same trains, confronting overcrowding, delays, and limited station facilities while still relying on these services as the most practical transportation option. These experiences highlight the need for system-wide improvements while also demonstrating the patience, endurance, and adaptability of those who depend on public transit every day (Buduan, 2018; Top Gear Philippines, 2019; Castillo, 2019)

Sources:
Buduan, K. (2016, December 24). MRT v LRT: A comparison of what it is like to ride both. Medium.
https://kenrickbuduan.medium.com/mrt-v-lrt-a-comparison-of-what-is-it-like-to-ride-both-3383acb6a488

Carlos, O. (2021, March 23). The prayer train. Medium.
https://oliverjetcastillo.medium.com/the-prayer-train-71060b5542c2

The realities of riding the MRT daily. (2019, August 27). Top Gear Philippines.
https://www.topgear.com.ph/features/feature-articles/realities-riding-mrt-daily-a4354-20190827

fields.title: History of the Rail System in the Philippines
fields.excerpt: The Philippines' rail system began in 1892 and evolved through colonial expansion, wartime damage, and modern urban rail projects.
fields.raw_text:
BLOG 5: History of the Rail System in the Philippines

Picture: history-of-the-rail-system-in-the-philippines.jpg
https://blogger.googleusercontent.com/img/b/R29vZ2xl/AVvXsEhpSYX_maTq2o1i1AbpitW7dLEwwrt8y6nq4swaEwIzAiyKlkx6MVAygXm1RqrEcZfBH6w8o8s75n5uGypWKkE3evjG11bcyStoHLR3g0E06L95bIHaESJj0d8Xj3dYm-qT7TBq0kFXXwpy/s1600/pnr-manila-and-rizal.jpg

The history of the rail system in the Philippines stretches back to the late 19th century, when the first steam-powered railway was inaugurated on November 24, 1892. Nearly two decades earlier, King Alfonso XII of Spain issued a decree for the establishment of a railway system in Luzon, prompting the construction of the Ferrocarril de Manila–Dagupan, which connected Manila to Dagupan in Pangasinan over a 195-kilometer track (Inquirer News, 2018; Philippine Railway Historical Society, n.d.). In those early years, railways and streetcar systems, locally called tranvías, served both commuters and freight, while electric tramways, introduced in 1905, circled Manila and served outlying districts (Sinfin, n.d.; Inquirer Opinion, 2016). Extension lines in Manila also reached Paco, Pasig, Marikina, Antipolo, Parañaque, and Bacoor, though these were destroyed during World War II and never rebuilt (History-Ph, 2017).

During the American colonial period, the Manila Railroad Company (MRRCo), which later became the Philippine National Railways (PNR), expanded the national rail network. From the Tutuban Central Station in Manila, construction moved north to Dagupan, and a new line was built from Bulacan to Cabanatuan in 1902. Subsequent expansions extended lines to La Union in the north and Albay in the south, creating a system that ultimately stretched approximately 1,100 kilometers from San Fernando, La Union, to Legazpi, Albay (Inquirer News, 2018; Sinfin, n.d.). Manila’s tranvía networks complemented these mainline services by facilitating intra-city travel.

World War II caused significant damage to the rail system nationwide, leaving much of the infrastructure in ruins (Inquirer Opinion, 2016). In the decades following the war, many lines fell into disuse due to neglect, mismanagement, and natural calamities. In 1964, MRRCo was formally converted into PNR through Republic Act No. 4156, becoming a government-owned corporation (Inquirer News, 2018). Although the network once linked La Union to Bicol, only limited segments remained operational by the late 20th century.

Railways also played a vital role in regional economies. During the Commonwealth period, imported steam locomotives transported sugar cane in Negros from haciendas to sugar mills, using main and lateral lines to service the milling districts (Inquirer News, 2018). Panay Railways Inc. operated a 117-kilometer line connecting Iloilo City to Roxas City in Capiz until financial difficulties led to its closure in the mid-1980s. In 1906, the Philippine Commission authorized the construction and operation of additional lines in other parts of the country, though many of these projects were short-lived (Inquirer News, 2018).

Urbanization and commuter demand in Metro Manila prompted renewed focus on modern urban rail transit. The Light Rail Transit Authority (LRTA) was created in 1980, leading to the opening of LRT-1 in 1984, the country’s first modern mass transit rail line (LRTA, n.d.). The Metro Rail Transit Line 3 (MRT-3), or Metrostar Express, was inaugurated in 1999, providing a critical north-south connection along EDSA (History-Ph, 2017). Today, urban lines such as LRT-1, LRT-2, and MRT-3 serve thousands of commuters daily, while planned projects like the North-South Commuter Railway aim to restore intercity and provincial rail connectivity (Sinfin, n.d.; Inquirer Opinion, 2016). The Philippine Railway Historical Society continues to preserve the historic legacy of the country’s rail system (Philippine Railway Historical Society, n.d.).

Sources:
History-Ph. (2017, April 21). Behind the names: Stations of MRT Line 3.
https://history-ph.blogspot.com/2017/04/mrt-3-names.html

Light Rail Transit Authority. (n.d.). LRTA history.
https://www.lrta.gov.ph/lrta-history/

Philippine Railway Historical Society. (n.d.). Hondagua - Beauty & The Beast.
https://philippinerailwayhistoricalsociety.blogspot.com/

Sinfin. (n.d.). Railways in the Philippines.
https://www.sinfin.net/railways/world/philippines.html

Butuyan, J. R. (2016, December 26). Modern train system of our past. Inquirer Opinion.
https://opinion.inquirer.net/100295/modern-train-system-past

Inquirer News. (2018, April 23). In the know: History of railroads in PH.
https://newsinfo.inquirer.net/984438/in-the-know-history-of-railroads-in-ph
body_paragraphs: The history of the rail system in the Philippines stretches back to the late 19th century, when the first steam-powered railway was inaugurated on November 24, 1892. Nearly two decades earlier, King Alfonso XII of Spain issued a decree for the establishment of a railway system in Luzon, prompting the construction of the Ferrocarril de Manila–Dagupan, which connected Manila to Dagupan in Pangasinan over a 195-kilometer track (Inquirer News, 2018; Philippine Railway Historical Society, n.d.). In those early years, railways and streetcar systems, locally called tranvías, served both commuters and freight, while electric tramways, introduced in 1905, circled Manila and served outlying districts (Sinfin, n.d.; Inquirer Opinion, 2016). Extension lines in Manila also reached Paco, Pasig, Marikina, Antipolo, Parañaque, and Bacoor, though these were destroyed during World War II and never rebuilt (History-Ph, 2017).

During the American colonial period, the Manila Railroad Company (MRRCo), which later became the Philippine National Railways (PNR), expanded the national rail network. From the Tutuban Central Station in Manila, construction moved north to Dagupan, and a new line was built from Bulacan to Cabanatuan in 1902. Subsequent expansions extended lines to La Union in the north and Albay in the south, creating a system that ultimately stretched approximately 1,100 kilometers from San Fernando, La Union, to Legazpi, Albay (Inquirer News, 2018; Sinfin, n.d.). Manila’s tranvía networks complemented these mainline services by facilitating intra-city travel.

World War II caused significant damage to the rail system nationwide, leaving much of the infrastructure in ruins (Inquirer Opinion, 2016). In the decades following the war, many lines fell into disuse due to neglect, mismanagement, and natural calamities. In 1964, MRRCo was formally converted into PNR through Republic Act No. 4156, becoming a government-owned corporation (Inquirer News, 2018). Although the network once linked La Union to Bicol, only limited segments remained operational by the late 20th century.

Railways also played a vital role in regional economies. During the Commonwealth period, imported steam locomotives transported sugar cane in Negros from haciendas to sugar mills, using main and lateral lines to service the milling districts (Inquirer News, 2018). Panay Railways Inc. operated a 117-kilometer line connecting Iloilo City to Roxas City in Capiz until financial difficulties led to its closure in the mid-1980s. In 1906, the Philippine Commission authorized the construction and operation of additional lines in other parts of the country, though many of these projects were short-lived (Inquirer News, 2018).

Urbanization and commuter demand in Metro Manila prompted renewed focus on modern urban rail transit. The Light Rail Transit Authority (LRTA) was created in 1980, leading to the opening of LRT-1 in 1984, the country’s first modern mass transit rail line (LRTA, n.d.). The Metro Rail Transit Line 3 (MRT-3), or Metrostar Express, was inaugurated in 1999, providing a critical north-south connection along EDSA (History-Ph, 2017). Today, urban lines such as LRT-1, LRT-2, and MRT-3 serve thousands of commuters daily, while planned projects like the North-South Commuter Railway aim to restore intercity and provincial rail connectivity (Sinfin, n.d.; Inquirer Opinion, 2016). The Philippine Railway Historical Society continues to preserve the historic legacy of the country’s rail system (Philippine Railway Historical Society, n.d.).

fields.title: How railway stations can transform urban mobility and the public realm: The stakeholders’ perspective
fields.authors: Lunardon, A., Vladimirova, D., Boucsein, B.
fields.snippet: Railway stations are massive infrastructures through which people, products, materials, and energy flow every day. They usually gather a multitude of functions and provide a wide range of services to users based on their respective specific features. Thus, railway stations have the potential to play a critical role in reshaping our cities in a sustainable manner by facilitating intermodality, green and active modes of transport and logistics, and by gathering proximity services. In this reshaping process, implementing effective and seamless mobility, as well as the proximity of services, are key challenges. However, no urban transformation process can take place without the involvement and commitment of the respective stakeholders. This paper aims to form an understanding of the views these stakeholders have towards the potential of railway stations transformation, for the broad European context, to carve out first paths towards actually achieving that transformation. This study analyses a wide range of inputs and considerations made during a series of workshops held in 2021 by the EIT Urban Mobility where experts from a wide range of fields exchanged their experiences and ideas around the topics of urban mobility and public realm. In this process, railway stations emerged as a key player to meet the challenges of cities' sustainable development. After analysing their potential and exploring policy obstacles that are currently hampering such a transformation, this paper suggests a series of recommendations to better exploit railway stations, gained from the stakeholders' perspective.

fields.title: Enhancing accessibility through rail transit in congested urban areas: A cross-regional analysis
fields.authors: Zhang, H., Zhan, B., Ouyang, M.
fields.snippet: The efficacy of urban rail transit in reducing the impact of road congestion is the subject of debate in policy and academic communities. Despite huge challenges arising from multifaceted factors such as data limitation, the recently released annual average congestion data enables us to empirically analyze the effect of urban rail transits in terms of their contribution to the average accessibility under road congestion. This paper presents a framework to evaluate the accessibility improvement benefit of urban rail transit under road congestion from a mean-field perspective. A cross-regional analysis in 43 Chinese cities demonstrates that the rail transit networks enhance the potential accessibility and contour accessibility by up to 5.07% and 12.09%, respectively. Besides, the accessibility improvement benefit largely depends on the road congestion level, rail network size, and layout of rail network. Furthermore, simulations on randomly generated rail transit networks indicate that the population coverage and proximity to roads are two structural determinants on the performance of rail transit. The proposed framework, together with the empirical findings, provides insights for city authorities to plan urban rail networks.

fields.title: The Association between Urban Public Transport Infrastructure and Social Equity and Spatial Accessibility within the Urban Environment: An Investigation of Tramlink in London
fields.authors: Cuthill, N., Cao, M., Liu, Y., Gao, X., Zhang, Y.
fields.snippet: The pursuit of sustainability has been at the forefront of contemporary planning initiatives. However, most recent research has focused on the environmental and economic aspects of developing sustainable urban environment, whilst largely neglecting the social aspects. Contemporary political thinking in the UK often disregards the potential of the urban infrastructure to improve social equity. The aim of this study was to analyse the impact of transport infrastructure on a variety of social measures, in an empirical and ideologically unbiased fashion, using both quantitative and qualitative methods. We selected Tramlink as a case study: a light-rail system in the London Borough of Croydon which began operation in 2000. We used quantitative methods, including advanced spatial statistics, to produce a more detailed analysis of social equity than has been previously published. This acknowledges that determining localised issues can produce more informed and effective policy interventions. Our results demonstrate that the physical properties of transport infrastructure and the non-physical attributes of society, in combination, help to create opportunities for individuals to succeed. We also find that in order to reduce the negative effects of austerity, public money could be more effectively spent if diverted to areas that are most in need which can be highlighted through localised investigations.

fields.title: Governing urban accessibility: moving beyond transport and mobility
fields.snippet: Access to people, goods, ideas and services is the basis of economic development in cities. The better this access, the greater the economic benefits through economies of scale, agglomeration effects and networking advantages. The way in which cities facilitate accessibility also impacts directly on other key aspects of human development, social inclusion and well-being. Accessibility is created through a complex interplay of urban form and transport systems. Thus, governing urban accessibility requires moving beyond conventional urban transport considerations linked to mobility and movement. Such a re-framing implies a far greater recognition of urban form characteristics like land use, distribution of densities and urban design, in addition to transport characteristics like infrastructures, service levels and travel speeds. A new interface between these characteristics has emerged as a result of shared mobility systems, putting additional pressure on city governments to act as system integrators. Based on a literature review, empirical insights from a global survey and the case-study cities of London, NYC and Berlin, this paper explores the institutional capacities of shifting from governing urban transport to urban accessibility. The evidence shows that there are entrenched misalignments which may impact negatively on the capacity to pair planning and policies essential for delivering better accessibility. Furthermore, it is clear that hierarchies and networks are not mutually exclusive when it comes to integrated governance of accessibility. The findings also suggest that cities may be better equipped to integrate shared mobility and consider mobility as a service than to pursue more wide-ranging metropolitan accessibility policies.

fields.title: The role of railway in handling transport services of cities and agglomerations
fields.snippet: The article discusses the changes in handling transport services of cities and agglomerations and the importance of railway in this aspect. Considerations on the transformation of the transport market, being the effect of changes on the market of goods and services and economic growth, the reasons for using railway transport in servicing urban and agglomeration traffic were identified. For many years the features characterizing rail transport have constituted a determinant of its use for the long-distance transport of homogeneous large cargo mass and long-distance transport of a significant number of passengers. The economic growth, and thus the increase in requirements on the transport market, caused a significant development of road transport in the short term. However, due to the negative external effects generated by this transport, as well as the change in the transport policy of individual countries and regions, steps have been taken towards the inter-branch diversification of transport services. Together with the dissemination of the concept of sustainable development, protection of the interests of future generations and limitation of emissions of harmful substances into the natural environment, innovative concepts, products and services to meet the tasks of a modern and responsible market appeared. The purpose of this article was to answer the following question: Is there a possibility for rail transport to be an important element of sustainable urban transport systems? Therefore, the article presents the concept of using an innovative system of self-propelled train compositions for handling transport services of cities and agglomerations. These train compositions, due to their limited length, their own propulsion system, and the control cabins located at both sides of the train, can be an alternative for handling feeder and transfer services in the agglomeration area, previously implemented mainly by means of road transport. The research area was Szczecin agglomeration with the city of Szczecin. Considerations and inferences were made on the basis of literature analysis, documentary method, external observation and methods of analysis and synthesis. This publication and/or research have been partially financed under the grant No 3/S/IZT/2018 financed from a subsidy of the Ministry of Science and Higher Education for statutory activities.
